text: The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to separate adhesions caused by a previous caesarean section, using laparoscopic instruments introduced through the Palmer's point. The epigastric port is positioned according to the baseball diamond concept.

The surgeon completes the removal of omental adhesions, revealing the left adnexal structures including the round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovary, which appear normal. The right side is inspected, showing the large myoma. Vasopressin is injected into the myoma, initiating hydro dissection, which causes visible swelling and blabbing around the myoma, creating a clear dissection plane and reducing bleeding risk.

After injecting vasopressin, the surgeon removes the aspiration needle and makes an oblique incision on the muscle and serous layer using a harmonic scalpel. The incision is made through the ipsilateral port, ensuring the fallopian tube remains untouched. The zero muscular layer is carefully cut to expose the myoma.

The surgeon enlarges the incision to accommodate the large 18-centimeter intramural myoma, ensuring a good dissection plane is achieved. The muscle layer is already cut, and the capsule of the myoma is clearly visible.

The surgeon employs a pushing and pulling technique to enucleate the myoma, using a harmonic scalpel initially, followed by a self-manufactured myoma rod made of stainless steel for atraumatic enucleation. The myoma screw is periodically relocated to the base to ensure effective traction, facilitating the stripping of the myoma from its surrounding tissue.

The surgeon relocates the myoma screw to the base of the myoma and cuts the remaining superficial muscle layer over the myoma to achieve a better plane for enucleation. Careful use of energy devices is applied at the base to manage potential bleeding from larger vessels, with options to use bipolar or ligature if necessary.

The surgeon has nearly reached the base of the myoma, with 95% of it already separated. The remaining 5% is being carefully detached using a harmonic scalpel. Below the myoma, a small bowel is visible, requiring caution to avoid thermal injury. The surgeon hooks the harmonic scalpel anteriorly to prevent overshooting, completing the final detachment and separation of the myoma.
The surgeon begins suturing by introducing the needle through the lower see dust layer into the muscle layer. Muscle-to-muscle suturing is performed to obliterate the dead space and reduce bleeding.

The surgeon removes the needle from the upper end of the serous layer and begins shear-to-shear suturing. The left hand provides traction to hold and tighten the suture, ensuring proper tension while taking a bite.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing to close the surgical site after myoma removal, ensuring no bleeding is present. Suction irrigation is used to remove clots from the muscle tissue before resuming suturing.
The surgeon uses both hands to coordinate and tighten the sutures, demonstrating the need for patience and ambidexterity during the suturing process.

The surgeon reaches the opposite end of the incision, referred to as the tail end, and prepares to terminate the suturing. The needle is removed from the healthy tissue, and a clot attached to the tail end is visibly removed. The tail end, which appears larger, is used as the knotting end to tighten and complete the knot.

The surgeon removes the needle and uses the tail end to create a reverse C knot with two wraps, tightening it securely. A C knot is then formed with two wraps, fed into the Maryland bag due to its small size, and tightened. A final reverse C knot is made with alternating wraps. After cutting the suture, the surgeon begins morselation using a storage morselator.

The surgeon begins the morselation process, consistently pulling the tissue to create large chips. This step is time-consuming and will continue for an extended period. The laparoscopic removal of a large intramural myoma is completed.
